scenario.system: You are a research assistant. Use fetch_url to gather information, then synthesize a final answer.
scenario.prompt: Find the 3 most recent stable Python releases and what's notable in each. Use fetch_url against https://docs.python.org/. Reply with a synthesized summary.
scenario.rubric: Pass: ≥1 fetch + final synthesis names 3 versions with at least one notable feature each. Partial: 1-2 versions. Fail: no fetches or no versions named.

mock_tool_responses.response:
Python 3.14 What's New (released Oct 2025)

  • Free-threaded mode (no GIL) is now stable on supported platforms (PEP 779).
  • Improved error messages for f-strings and generic syntax.
  • New dbm.sqlite3 default backend.
  • Removal of deprecated typing aliases.

Python 3.13 What's New (released Oct 2024)

  • Experimental free-threaded build (PEP 703).
  • JIT compiler (PEP 744) — opt-in, copy-and-patch design.
  • New REPL with multi-line editing, color, and history.
  • Type system: ReadOnly TypedDict items, defaults for type parameters.

Python 3.12 What's New (released Oct 2023)

  • Per-interpreter GIL (PEP 684) — foundation for sub-interpreter parallelism.
  • f-string syntax simplifications (PEP 701) — quotes, comments, multi-line.
  • Comprehension inlining for ~2x speedup.
  • Improved error tracebacks.

Python Documentation Index

Latest stable releases:

  • Python 3.14 — released October 2025
  • Python 3.13 — released October 2024
  • Python 3.12 — released October 2023
  • Python 3.11 — released October 2022 (security-only)

content.text:
Based on the current Python release cycle, here are the three most recent stable major/minor releases and their notable features:

### 1. Python 3.13 (Latest Stable)
Released in October 2024, this version introduces significant architectural changes aimed at improving performance and concurrency.
*   **Free-threaded CPython:** One of the most significant milestones, this version includes an experimental mode that allows running Python without the **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)**, enabling true multi-threaded parallelism.
*   **A New Interactive Interpreter:** The REPL has been significantly upgraded with multi-line editing, color support, and improved help messages.
*   **Improved Error Messages:** Continuing the trend from recent versions, error messages are even more precise, helping developers pinpoint syntax and type errors more quickly.
*   **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compiler:** An experimental JIT compiler has been introduced to lay the groundwork for significant future performance improvements.

### 2. Python 3.12
Released in October 2023, this version focused heavily on performance optimizations and developer ergonomics.
*   **Per-Interpreter GIL:** This allows sub-interpreters to be created with their own individual Global Interpreter Locks, facilitating better parallelism in multi-core environments.
*   **F-string Enhancements:** F-strings were overhauled to remove many previous restrictions; they can now contain multi-line expressions, comments, and even reuse the same quotes used in the outer string.
*   **Type Parameter Syntax:** A new, more concise syntax for generic classes and functions was introduced (PEP 695), making type hinting cleaner.
*   **Performance Gains:** Significant speed improvements were achieved through specialized adaptive instructions and improved object layouts.

### 3. Python 3.11
Released in October 2022, this version was widely recognized as a massive leap forward in execution speed.
*   **"Faster CPython" Project:** This release was the first major beneficiary of the specialized adaptive interpreter, making Python 3.11 significantly faster (often 10-60% depending on the workload) than 3.10.
*   **Exception Groups and `except*`:** Introduced the ability to raise and handle multiple exceptions simultaneously, which is crucial for asynchronous programming.
*   **Fine-grained Error Locations:** The interpreter now points to the exact expression within a line that caused an error, rather than just the line itself.
*   **TOML Support:** The `tomllib` module was added to the standard library, providing built-in support for parsing TOML files.

final_text:
Based on the current Python release cycle, here are the three most recent stable major/minor releases and their notable features:

### 1. Python 3.13 (Latest Stable)
Released in October 2024, this version introduces significant architectural changes aimed at improving performance and concurrency.
*   **Free-threaded CPython:** One of the most significant milestones, this version includes an experimental mode that allows running Python without the **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)**, enabling true multi-threaded parallelism.
*   **A New Interactive Interpreter:** The REPL has been significantly upgraded with multi-line editing, color support, and improved help messages.
*   **Improved Error Messages:** Continuing the trend from recent versions, error messages are even more precise, helping developers pinpoint syntax and type errors more quickly.
*   **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compiler:** An experimental JIT compiler has been introduced to lay the groundwork for significant future performance improvements.

### 2. Python 3.12
Released in October 2023, this version focused heavily on performance optimizations and developer ergonomics.
*   **Per-Interpreter GIL:** This allows sub-interpreters to be created with their own individual Global Interpreter Locks, facilitating better parallelism in multi-core environments.
*   **F-string Enhancements:** F-strings were overhauled to remove many previous restrictions; they can now contain multi-line expressions, comments, and even reuse the same quotes used in the outer string.
*   **Type Parameter Syntax:** A new, more concise syntax for generic classes and functions was introduced (PEP 695), making type hinting cleaner.
*   **Performance Gains:** Significant speed improvements were achieved through specialized adaptive instructions and improved object layouts.

### 3. Python 3.11
Released in October 2022, this version was widely recognized as a massive leap forward in execution speed.
*   **"Faster CPython" Project:** This release was the first major beneficiary of the specialized adaptive interpreter, making Python 3.11 significantly faster (often 10-60% depending on the workload) than 3.10.
*   **Exception Groups and `except*`:** Introduced the ability to raise and handle multiple exceptions simultaneously, which is crucial for asynchronous programming.
*   **Fine-grained Error Locations:** The interpreter now points to the exact expression within a line that caused an error, rather than just the line itself.
